content.full_text:
RAVl MEHTA 
CHAIRMAN 
\ 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
April 25, 1997 
Mark Shaw 
California League of Conservation Voters 
Political Action for Environmental Protection 
965 Mission Street, Suite 625 
San Francisco, California 94103 
Re: Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. 1-97-125 
Dear Mr. Shaw: 
This letter is a response to your request for advice on behalf of the California League of 
Conservation Voters ("League") and its affiliated local chapters regarding the provisions of the 
Political Refonn Act (the "Act").! Because your request does not identify a specific advisee, we 
are treating this as a request for infonnal assistance.
2 
QUESTIONS 
1. Are boardmembers of a local chapter affiliated with the League prohibited from 
volunteering to do campaign work for a candidate if the League endorses the candidate? Does 
the answer change if that boardmember, or any boardmember ofthat local chapter, participates in 
the endorsement process which leads to the League's endorsement of that candidate? 
2. If a local chapter of the League is actively involved in a candidate's campaign, may 
the League make an independent expenditure on behalf of that candidate? 
1 Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations. 
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3), (c)(4).) 
428 J STREET, SUITE 800. SACRAMRNTn r A' ''''nnw. """0' A 
,~.-. 


CONCLUSIONS 
File No. 1-97-125 
Page 2 
If a boardmember of a local chapter provides volunteer personal services to a candidate, 
the services are not considered a contribution to the candidate and do not need to be reported. 
This remains the case even if the League endorses the candidate, and even if local boardmembers 
participate in League's endorsement process. 
A local chapter may endorse a candidate or generate volunteers for a candidate, and 
individual boardmembers may volunteer on behalf of a candidate without affecting the League's 
ability to make independent expenditures on behalf of that candidate. However, a local chapter's 
monetary contribution to a candidate may affect the League's ability to make independent 
expenditures on behalf of that same candidate. 
FACTS 
The League and its local chapters are affiliated by virtue of the fact that each local chapter 
has at least one member of its highest governing body who also serves as a staffperson of the 
League. In addition, some chapters share membership with the League and some chapters are 
subject to some degree of oversight by the League. However, no representatives of local 
chapters are members of the League's board of directors, and local chapters have no oversight 
regarding the League's endorsement of candidates or independent expenditures. 
The League has an endorsement process by which the League decides which candidates 
to support. This process involves sending out a questionnaire to candidates, reviewing the 
questionnaires, and then interviewing candidates either at the League's offices or by phone. 
Those who participate in the endorsement process include the League's boardmembers and staff, 
as well as members of the local chapters. Participants sit in on the interviews, and recommend to 
the endorsement committee which candidates to endorse. Even if members of the local chapters 
participate in the process that leads to the fonnal endorsement by the League, those local 
members have no input as to which candidates the League ultimately commits its resources. The 
final decision rests solely with the League. 
Lastly, the local chapters may be actively involved in a particular candidate's campaign. 
This activity would include endorsing the candidate, making contributions to the candidate, and 
generating volunteers in support of the candidate. Some boardmembers (not including League 
staff) volunteer their time as individuals. 
ANALYSIS 
Question #1 
The contribution limits of Proposition 208 apply to both monetary and in-kind 
contributions of goods or services. However, Section 82015 specifically states that the tenn 


ANALYSIS 
Question #1 
File No. 1-97-125 
Page 3 
The contribution limits of Proposition 208 apply to both monetary and in-kind 
contributions of goods or services. However, Section 82015 specifically states that the term 
contribution "does not include volunteer personal services .... " (See also Regulation 18215 
(c )(2).) Therefore, if a boardmember of a local chapter provides volunteer personal services to a 
candidate, the services are not considered a contribution to the candidate and do not need to be 
reported.3 
Proposition 208 places limitations upon campaign contributions and when such 
contributions may be accepted. An endorsement does not fit within the definition of 
contribution. (See Sections 82015, 82044; Regulation 18215.) Therefore, there is no prohibition 
restricting boardmembers oflocal chapters from volunteering for a candidate in their individual 
capacities, even if the League endorses the candidate. This remains the case even if the local 
boardmembers participate in the League's endorsement process in the manner you have 
described. 
Question #2 
You have indicated that a local chapter could be "actively involved" in a candidate's 
campaign in a number of ways. The local chapter might endorse a candidate or generate 
volunteers. Its boardmembers may volunteer their time as individuals. Lastly, the local chapter 
may make monetary contributions to a candidate. You have asked whether any of these activities 
by a local chapter in support of a candidate would have an impact on the League's ability to 
make independent expenditures on behalf of that candidate. 
Again, nothing in the Act regulates or prohibits the local chapter's endorsement of 
candidates. Nor does the Act regulate individual boardmembers who choose to volunteer their 
time. Likewise, it does not prohibit a local chapter from generating volunteers for a particular 
candidate. Therefore, none of these activities in support of a candidate on the part of local 
chapters will affect the League's ability to make independent expenditures on behalf of that 
candidate. 
However, a local chapter's monetary contribution to a candidate may affect the League's 
ability to make independent expenditures on behalf of that same candidate. Section 85500 
provides, in part: 
"(c) Any contributor that makes a contribution of one hundred 
3 You should be aware that if the League or its local chapters compensate volunteers, the compensation 
may be deemed to be a contribution. (See Regulation 18423.) Because your letter does not indicate that volunteers 
will be compensated, this issue is not analyzed in this letter. 


File No. 1-97-125 
Page 4 
dollars ($100) or more per election to a candidate for elective office shall 
be considered to be acting in concert with that candidate and shall not make 
independent expenditures and contributions which in combination exceed 
the amounts set forth in Section 85301 in support of that candidate or in 
opposition to that candidate's opponent or opponents." 
Section 85311 provides that: 
"All payments made by a person established, financed, maintained, 
or controlled by any business entity, labor organization, labor organization, 
association, political party, or any other person or group of such persons 
shall be considered to be made by a single person." 
Because the local chapters are affiliated with the League within the definition of Section 
85311, any contribution by the local chapters will be considered to be made by the aggregate of 
the League and all of its local chapters. Therefore, Section 85500 would limit the League's 
ability to make independent expenditures if the aggregate of its local chapters' contributions to a 
candidate exceed $100. 
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660. 
Sincerely, 
Steven G. Churchwell 
G 
.~ 
eborah Allison 
Staff Counsel, Legal Division 
SGC:DA:ak

written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3), (c)(4).)

participate in League's endorsement process.

individual boardmembers may volunteer on behalf of a candidate without affecting the League's 
ability to make independent expenditures on behalf of that candidate. However, a local chapter's 
monetary contribution to a candidate may affect the League's ability to make independent 
expenditures on behalf of that same candidate.
